title: Locate Fire Extinguisher
description: Find the fire extinguisher in the main lobby. Fire extinguishers should be used for small, contained fires only.

details.text: Fire extinguishers are crucial safety equipment designed to put out small fires before they spread. They are typically mounted on walls in visible, easily accessible locations.
When using a fire extinguisher, remember the P.A.S.S technique: Pull the pin, Aim at the base of fire, Squeeze the handle, and Sweep from side to side.

Fire extinguishers are color-coded based on the type of fires they can handle: Class A (ordinary combustibles), Class B (flammable liquids), Class C (electrical equipment), and Class D (flammable metals).

title: Find Emergency Exit
description: Locate the nearest emergency exit sign. Always know your evacuation routes in case of a fire emergency.

details.text: Emergency exit signs are illuminated and typically found above doorways leading to safe exit routes. They are designed to be visible even in smoke-filled or dark conditions.
In an emergency, always use stairs instead of elevators. Elevators may stop functioning or become dangerous during a fire.
When evacuating, stay low to avoid smoke inhalation, feel doors for heat before opening, and proceed calmly to the nearest exit.

title: Locate Fire Alarm
description: Find the fire alarm pull station. In case of fire, pull the alarm to alert others in the building.

details.text: Fire alarm pull stations are typically small, red boxes mounted on walls near exits. They allow anyone to manually activate the building's fire alarm system.
When you discover a fire, first ensure your own safety, then activate the nearest fire alarm pull station by pulling down the lever. This alerts everyone in the building to evacuate.
After activating the fire alarm, exit the building immediately. Do not attempt to gather belongings or use elevators.
False alarms can cause panic and waste emergency resources. Only activate the fire alarm in a genuine emergency situation.

title: Cardboard Recycling
description: Find the cardboard recycling bin. Cardboard boxes should be flattened before disposal to save space.

details.text: Cardboard is one of the most recyclable materials. When properly recycled, it can be transformed into new cardboard products in as little as two weeks.
Before recycling cardboard, remove any tape, labels, or other non-paper materials. Flatten boxes to save space in the recycling bin.
Cardboard contaminated with food (like pizza boxes with grease) cannot be recycled and should be composted or disposed of in general waste.
For every ton of cardboard recycled, we save about 17 trees, 7,000 gallons of water, and 4,000 kilowatts of electricity.

title: Recyclable Materials
description: Locate the recycling bin for plastic, glass, and metal items. These materials can be recycled multiple times to conserve resources.

details.text: Common recyclable materials include plastic bottles (types 1 and 2), glass containers, aluminum cans, steel cans, and clean paper products.
Always rinse containers before recycling to remove food residue. Contaminated recycling can cause entire batches to be sent to landfill.
Look for recycling numbers on plastic items. Not all plastics are recyclable in all areas - check local guidelines.
Metal cans are infinitely recyclable without loss of quality. Recycling one aluminum can saves enough energy to power a TV for three hours.
Glass bottles can be recycled endlessly. Recycling one glass bottle saves enough energy to power a computer for 30 minutes.

title: General Waste
description: Find the general waste bin for non-recyclable items. Always try to minimize waste that ends up in landfills.

details.text: General waste typically includes non-recyclable plastics (types 3-7 in many regions), contaminated paper products, disposable diapers, and certain composite materials.
Remember the waste hierarchy: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle. The best approach is to reduce consumption of items that will become waste.
Consider alternatives to common waste items: use reusable shopping bags, refillable water bottles, and products with minimal packaging.
Hazardous waste such as batteries, electronics, paints, and certain chemicals should never go in general waste. They require special disposal methods.
The average person generates about 4.5 pounds of waste per day. By being mindful of consumption and disposal habits, we can significantly reduce this number.
